data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Toast, Archer Aviation, Klaviyo And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between Sep. 26 and Oct. 2.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Klaviyo Is A Sleeping Giant (Strong Buy Initiation)

PropNotes | Strong Buy

Klaviyo (NYSE:KVYO) is a stock that came across our radar recently. It’s a B2B SaaS company that provides a B2C CRM product for businesses. CRM, for those who don’t know, stands for 'customer relationship management.' Historically, CRM software has been geared towards the B2B market, as sales teams have needed a way to keep track of their deals and prospects.

Now, however, as we advance into an increasingly digital age, having one central place where a business can market, sell, and service retail customers makes a lot of sense. With one integrated platform that maps directly to three of the seven key business functions, KVYO's platform has an incredibly strong value proposition.

In recent years, the company has performed spectacularly, growing revenues like clockwork while maintaining robust gross margins. And yet, since the IPO in 2023, shares have traded down.

We don’t think this makes a lot of sense.

Today, we'll dive deeper into KVYO's business, highlight the valuation, and make the case that this B2B SaaS firm belongs in your portfolio.

Read more here...

Palo Alto Networks $19B Surprise Few See Coming

Uttam Dey | Buy

Early last month, cybersecurity heavyweight Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW) crashed ~18% after announcing its largest acquisition ever—a $25B takeover of cybersecurity access security firm CyberArk (NASDAQ:CYBR).

The acquisition announcement by the cybersecurity giant from Palo Alto, CA, initially received flak with some analysts citing the lack of “meaningful synergies” in the acquisition and downgraded Palo Alto Networks shares or PANW.

At the time, I was already in the process of securing some exposure towards PANW’s shares in my portfolio and the 18% drop in its shares looked like an adverse over-reaction to the acquisition news.

My thesis for buying PANW’s shares was simple—that it was “perfectly positioned to enter new growth markets,” as I noted in my previous research, and markets were, simultaneously discounting PANW’s ability to keep up its +35% FCF margins and its industry-leading revenue run rates, supported by backlog that is expected to swell to $19B next year.

PANW’s updates from the past 30-60 days have further deepened my conviction in its shares, and I continue to recommend a Buy on its shares.

Read more here...

W. P. Carey: Separation From The Others Is Just Beginning

Julian Lin | Buy

W. P. Carey (NYSE:WPC) is finally starting to trade at a premium to its historical comparable peers, but that’s by intention. The company has successfully transformed its portfolio to focus on industrial and warehouse properties, which have superior economics and resiliency compared to the traditional retail or office model. While capital recycling remains elevated due to the high interest rate environment, the company is still positioned for consistent and persistent growth. I see continued upside ahead, with investors enjoying a generous dividend yield as they wait. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

Read more here...

WPAY: A Weekly Paying Alternative To YMAX

Cain Lee | Buy

This is easily one of the best times in modern history to be an income investor. There is such an abundance of different funds that now pay distributions on a weekly basis and as time goes on, the funds have become more efficient in terms of stability. Roundhill WeeklyPay Universe ETF (BATS:WPAY) is the latest addition to my portfolio because I believe it has the potential to be the top performing weekly paying option ETF in the market. WPAY launched September 3rd, 2025 and is managed by Roundhill Investments. The fund has a very reasonable 0.99% expense ratio, considering that investors are getting a huge distribution yield in return.

Read more here...

MercadoLibre: Two Times Three Is $100 Billion

RB Equity | Strong Buy

MELI is the most valuable company in LatAm despite also being one of the youngest listed entities in the region, only turning 26 this year.

How is that possible?

LatAm is a region with over +600 million people (about ~2x US). With the three largest economies being Brazil (211 million, GDP $2.3 Trillion), Mexico (130 million, GDP $1.9 Trillion) and Argentina (45 million, GDP $650 Billion). The big three thus contain about 2/3 of non-English-speaking population in the continent and more than that in GDP share.

Holding a dominant position in the "LatAm big three" is a good first step to becoming the largest corporation. MELI did this already. The second step is to be in an industry with a very long runway for growth (and hence command premium multiples). MELI is there as well, not with one but with both of its businesses.

Read more here...

Archer Aviation: Takeoff Is Imminent

Bay Area Ideas | Buy

Air mobility is one of the next frontiers of advancement in transportation. The U.S. and China are in a race to deploy such technologies, but in late 2024, I decided that Archer Aviation Inc. (NYSE:ACHR) stock wasn't ready for takeoff yet. I initiated with a Sell rating, and since then Archer has slightly underperformed the S&P 500 (SP500). I believe giving an update now would be appropriate, as there have been many new developments for the company.

In the below analysis, I discover that the growth story is highly intact and many developments now show commercialization is right around the corner. While the company did dilute shareholders to strengthen their cash position, dilution risk is likely behind us for the near term. Furthermore, Archer's P/B ratio has contracted significantly from all-time highs, and so this is a great opportunity for investors. As a result, I have decided to double upgrade the stock to a buy rating.

Read more here...

GE Vernova: Higher For Longer Growth Rate

Ricardo Fernandez | Buy

One of my key sector focuses has been the broader electricity energy ecosystem, encompassing fuels (natural gas and uranium), independent power producers, construction companies, cable suppliers, and power plant or gas turbine manufacturers. All have done well as US load demand rises, driven by climate change, electrification, and data center demand. I began covering GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV) (NEOE:GEV:CA) in June 2024, citing the turnaround that was developing, and then followed up with a view on higher multiple potential. Now I update my coverage and maintain a buy on higher for longer growth.

Read more here...

Aya Gold & Silver: Free Cash Flow In Sight With Dazzlingly High Silver Prices

Alberto Abaterusso | Buy

This article recommends a BUY rating for Aya Gold & Silver Inc. (TSX:AYA:CA), a stock listed on the TSX exchange. This represents an upside from our previous analysis, which issued a hold rating on October 6, 2024. ...

AYA's shares, listed on the TSX (“Toronto Stock Exchange”), have fallen by -28.44% compared to the share price at the time of our previous analysis's Hold rating and have underperformed the S&P 500 (change +15.11%). Returns are as of this new article on AYA shares. The S&P 500 is the benchmark for the US stock market. In principle, AYA stock is an excellent long-term investment, as its performance on the OTCQX speaks volumes: if you compare this stock to its direct competitors over the long term, it is very likely to outperform most of them significantly.

Read more here...

Fiserv: A Value Investor's Golden Ticket

Arjun Pathy | Strong Buy

Management guidance cuts, product rollout issues, and a poorly timed CEO transition have spooked Fiserv (NYSE:FI) investors, resulting in a more than 40% YTD underperformance compared to fintech benchmark ETF FINX. Out of this overblown reaction, Fiserv has emerged as a cheap value play built on strong expansion in margin and revenues, an efficient capital structure, and a strategy of aggressively returning cash to shareholders. Going into Q2 earnings, FI traded at nearly 30x earnings, and despite an EPS beat, after management set annual revenue guidance at 10% (the low end of a previous 10-12% guidance), the stock fell to a 52-week low.

Sharp competition from Toast and Stripe, four concurrent acquisitions, and rollout delays across XD, Cash Flow Central, and Clover’s healthcare expansion compounded concerns. Such advancements would typically prompt a minor correction, but instead led to a dramatic retreat from all-time highs. Contrary to popular sentiment, strong fundamentals, especially when put into context with competitors, indicate that entering FI is a bargain at current prices.

Read more here...

Lineage: A Deeply Discounted Cold Storage Giant Positioned To Consolidate The Industry

Michael Duke | Buy

Lineage (NASDAQ:LINE) is the largest cold storage operator in the world. Like its closest peer, Americold (COLD), it owns and operates temperature-controlled warehouses that serve as mission-critical nodes in the global supply chain for food, pharmaceuticals, and other perishables. The business model combines long-lived infrastructure with active logistics operations, producing a unique REIT-like hybrid. But while Lineage has global scale and strategic importance, its stock has fallen over 50% from recent highs.

Despite this drawdown, Lineage remains a dominant force in the cold chain and may be even better positioned than Americold to benefit from the long-term growth and consolidation of the industry. The business faces real challenges, oversupply, cost inflation, and softening occupancy, but the stock now trades at a valuation that prices in these risks and leaves room for upside if the industry normalizes.

Read more here...

Bloom Energy: The Not Too Overvalued Bet Fueling The Data Center CapEx Boom

Sarfatti Investment Research | Strong Buy

I am initiating coverage on Bloom Energy (NYSE:BE) with a Strong Buy rating. BE is very well positioned in today’s market to deploy its fuel cell solutions to support the rapid data center CapEx expansion fueled by AI. Further exacerbating BE’s growth prospects is the current natural gas-forward energy mix in the US, with little propensity to change in the medium term. My thesis will be focused largely on data centers, as this is where growth is concentrated, with a brief aside on maritime developments.

Read more here...

Toast Won't Remain Cheap For Too Long

Amrita Roy | Buy

When I last wrote about Toast (NYSE:TOST), I upgraded the stock to a “buy,” as the company continued to see momentum in its core US market while closing its largest enterprise deal with Applebee’s, demonstrating the versatility of its verticalized product strategy.

While I have frequently pointed out that a slowing economy will put pressure on Toast, what amazed me was that the management raised their full-year forward guidance while continuing to operate with superior financial discipline.

Unfortunately the stock has dropped quite considerably since August and saw another round of downward volatility this past week as news emerged of the company reducing pricing for its software packages aimed at smaller restaurants.

Since then, the pricing has reverted back to its original levels, while its Q2 FY25 earnings continued to show rapid progress in Toast’s initiatives in expanding its TAM (total addressable market) across enterprise, retail, and international markets.

What was also optimistic was that management raised its full-year guidance even higher, defying concerns of macroeconomic pressures.

I believe the recent 25% drawdown in the stock price since August has created an extraordinary opportunity in the stock with a significantly higher risk-reward than before. I am reiterating my price target at $55.5 per share, which translates to a potential upside of 49% from its current levels, making it a “buy”.

Read more here...

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ouster: Taking Profits, But Shares Are Still A 'Hold'
data.description:
Introduction

A few years ago, I began researching robotics and autonomous technology, which, while still in their infancy, may be long-term trends that can disrupt society. Given the demographic trends of lower population growth and increasing aging, it seems to me that autonomous humanoid robotics can fill the worker gap. While autonomous vehicles are closer to implementation on scale, this can also dramatically disrupt transport and logistics ecosystems, i.e., from traffic, parking, and safety to lower fuel insurance and auto/truck demand. At the time, my views had a high dose of science fiction, and yet here we are with AI capacity rapidly making these technologies viable. This led me to lidar (laser imaging, detection, and ranging), or how human eyes will be replaced in robots and vehicles, and I bought shares in Ouster (NASDAQ:OUST) that have since performed above my expectations.

What is Ouster?

Founded in 2015 and IPOed during the SPAC bubble, the company has been slow to monetize its technology. Part of this is due to the high level of competition and focus on AV (autonomous technology) that has taken much longer to develop than envisioned. Along the way, the company expanded its market or created new uses for lidar and coupled it with AI (software) to finally begin production and a long road to operating breakeven. The Ouster investor believes in lidar and the company's ability to expand into multiple sectors. The skeptic points to the significant competition and Tesla’s (TSLA) disdain for Liar.

How Big is the Lidar Market?

Ouster believes its technology and products have a $70 billion addressable market that comprises robotics ($14 billion), industrial ($18 billion), smart infrastructure ($19 billion), and automotive ($20 billion). However, looking at three other sources, the size of the market seems substantially smaller, with the high end at $13 billion and the low end at $4.7 billion on a global basis. That said, the growth rates are very high at over 20% per year. The difference vs. Ouster is that they see many more applications for lidar/AI than.

The second factor of more concern is competition. It’s a crowded sector with many Chinese, U.S., and EU suppliers vying for a piece of the growing pie. Most are still lower tech, i.e., not incorporating AI/software or advancements into new sectors, and focused on automotives. Nonetheless, the technological capabilities should not be underestimated, nor should the same ability to expand the prediction to new use cases. Ouster will not be alone in this sector, and this may be the reason for a slower-than-suspected revenue run rate.

Operating Model

The current consensus estimates that OUST needs to reach about 50k units sold to break even, vs. the 22k unit rate of 2025. The gross margin or cost of production is solid at 45%, but revenue/production does not pay for opex (R&D, SGA), which needs to be diluted by scale/revenue growth. On the positive side, the company is gaining traction on various fronts for orders in many use cases and may see revenue acceleration far above consensus, which may be required to sustain the share price.

Financial Estimates

OUST, despite negative EBITDA and FCF assumed through 2027, has a solid cash position built via successful capital increases and diminishing cash burn. As mentioned earlier, R&D and its SGA structure costs are now mostly fixed, and added revenue should dilute with revenue growth. Capex needs are low since manufacturing is outsourced with Benchmark; thus, cash earnings (net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation) should turn positive in 2027 or sooner if OUST can reach the 50k units production level or $275 million in revenue.

Valuation

This is the hard part. When the stock was $5 or around $300 million in market cap and beginning to add revenue, valuation metrics were less relevant. Now at around $1.5 billion and C-suites selling positions, valuation based on medium-term fundamentals is becoming a requirement for share price momentum and stability.

The consensus has a $35 price target for YE26, or 9x revenue (since there is negative EBITDA). My approach with start-up or pre-earnings companies is to value the shares at a high multiple relative to cash earnings growth (when that is achieved) and assign a long-term price target that, if it results in at least a 15% annual return, may be worth holding. OUST, if it meets estimates, may be valued at $44 by YE28, assuming 2x PEG on 2028 forward growth. This is equal to 18% annual price gains.

Peer Comps

As mentioned earlier, this is a crowded and competitive sector, not that OUST can't or has not distinguished itself via technology, but many companies produce basic lidar for the automotive sector and increase autonomous vehicles. The more pure plays, such as Luminar (LAZR) and Aeva (AEVA), are far from break-even with volatile share prices. The auto parts makers are venturing into lidar. This is a high-risk sector with potential rapid growth but weak returns so far.

Risk

The company faces substantial risk from technological obsolescence, pricing wars vs. larger players, and the slow uptake of lidar. All could derail the path to break-even margins and a sustainable share price.

Conclusion

The company needs to meet high growth and margin targets to sustain its current share price and faces substantial volatility and competitive pressure. While the long-term upside may justify a "Buy," the combination of execution risk and high share price has led me to take profit and reduce the position. I rate OUST a "Hold."

data.title: Tapestry: Lots Of Positive Developments, But Kate Spade Remains A Drag
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on Tapestry (NYSE:TPR) was a hold rating because I believed the timing of revenue growth acceleration was not going to materialize in the near term. Fast forward to today, management has simplified the portfolio and set a clear path ahead at the 2025 Investor Day. The sale of Stuart Weitzman and the termination of the Capri Holdings acquisition have removed key overhangs, while Coach’s momentum is strong enough to anchor long-term growth. That said, Kate Spade remains a show-me story, and at 20x forward earnings, much of the near-term upside appears priced in. I stay on hold.

A More Focused Strategy Creates a Clearer Path Forward

Following my last update, the investment case for TPR has become much clearer, but I don’t think this is worth a buy rating today. I will go into more details later, but let’s start with the positive developments that are noteworthy.

At the 2025 Investor Day, management outlined a multi-year plan that directly addresses several overhangs I had previously flagged. Most notably, the portfolio has now been streamlined with the completed sale of Stuart Weitzman on August 4, 2025, removing a drag that had weighed on profitability. Also, the termination of the Capri Holdings acquisition has eliminated a major source of uncertainty around execution and synergies. Taken together, these moves simplify the narrative and shift the focus squarely back to organic growth, giving investors a cleaner story to underwrite.

The major piece of this reset is the “Amplify” plan, which reframes how TPR defines its market opportunity. There are some nuances here, but I think the implications are huge. Rather than focusing on dollar-based share, TPR is now targeting a consumer-based total addressable market of 1.9 billion people globally. At the core of this strategy is a focus on acquiring customers at the point of market entry, defined as 18- to 27-year-olds. This focus on the point of market entry consumer is important for several reasons.

First, it's a sizable and constantly refreshing market. During the presentation, management noted that every year for the next decade, 25 million women will turn 18 and look to make their first major handbag purchase. This provides a sustainable stream of new customers to the TPR. Second, getting customers when they're young is a big deal because that's when they form lasting habits. If their first nice bag is a Coach, they'll likely feel a connection to the brand for a long time, making them a very valuable customer over their lifetime. Plus, what's cool with this younger generation often becomes cool for everyone else, so their style choices can influence what older generations want to buy.

Evidently, the strategy is already proving effective, as these younger customers are not only growing as a cohort but are also more loyal. Management explicitly stated that Gen Z's year-one retention rate in North America was >150 bps higher than the average of other cohorts. Hence, by focusing on younger consumers with higher retention rates, TPR is effectively extending its growth runway with higher lifetime value across its brands. Also, for perspective, management expectation is for this cohort to drive ~85% of new customer acquisition through 2028.

Coach

Shift in focus aside, the Coach brand continues to do very well and should be the main support for TPR to invest in the younger generations. While I was cautious about revenue sustainability before, management’s updated outlook is more convincing. The three-year plan now calls for a mid-single-digit revenue CAGR, framed as a “floor,” alongside a longer-term goal of reaching more than $10 billion in sales. I am more confident in this guide because it is supported by the successful execution of the “One Coach” strategy, which integrates Collection products into the outlet channel. The impact on Average Unit Revenue has been powerful, and what makes the story even stronger is the recent inflection back to positive unit growth, despite lower promotional activity. That signals underlying consumer demand is healthy, not artificially supported.

Execution Hurdles

While the outlook for Coach is solid, not everything is rosy for TPR. Most notably the turnaround at Kate Spade. As I noted previously, Kate Spade’s performance has been “horrible,” and it still represents a classic show-me story. To management’s credit, a clearer, three-phase plan has now been laid out, modeled on the successful Coach playbook. The core issue is that Kate Spade is an "underused asset" (words by management). The brand lacks awareness and has low top-of-mind share. To put it simply, most people know the name, but they don't think of it first when making a purchase. The new strategy aims to fix this by focusing on three key things.

First, TPR will significantly ramp up investment to build brand heat, increasing marketing spend by 400 bps as a percentage of sales and boosting brand media investment by 60%. This effort will target a specific psychographic, the "Gen Z Connector," with the goal of increasing Gen Z penetration by 60% through 2028.

Second, the product assortment will be simplified to create focus, with handbag styles being reduced by 40%. I think this is a smart move, as it will allow the brand to invest in the in-demand products, which means the business model shifts to desire-driven.

Finally, it is to create a more cohesive consumer experience across all touchpoints.

In terms of cadence, FY26 will be the streamlining year with a modest operating loss, followed by better profitability and growth in FY27 and a full-scale-up year in FY28 with an acceleration to mid-single-digit growth and a high-single-digit operating margin. That said, as much as they sound great, it also confirms a period of near-term pain and places a heavy burden on execution. What if the same strategy cannot be replicated with Kate Spade because the brand is just not what Gen Z prefers? Then this entire turnaround effort will just be a drag to the P&L.

I think what we need to see is Kate Spade to show some form of positive traction in the coming quarters in order for the market to start looking into FY27/28. If not, I think this will continue to be a drag on TPR’s stock sentiment.

Higher Valuation Bar

Finally, valuation has risen to a level that was outside of my expectations previously. In my prior update, I viewed the stock as fairly valued and range-bound, weighed down by the Capri deal overhang. With that uncertainty cleared and Coach delivering strong fundamentals, the stock has understandably re-rated. But that also raises the bar. For reference, the stock trades at 20x forward PE. This is not cheap when compared to the historical range—20x is the +2 standard deviation level. At this valuation, I think the market has already priced in a good amount of upside over the near term. In other words, expectations are running hot, and there is little margin of safety. As I said, should the efforts with Kate Spade fail, the market will likely start to de-rate the multiple as the recovery timeline drags out.

Conclusion

I give TPR a hold rating. TPR has taken important steps to streamline its portfolio and sharpen its growth strategy, with Coach continuing to show durable brand strength and consumer demand. However, the multi-year turnaround at Kate Spade introduces real execution risk. With the stock already re-rated to the upper end of its historical multiple range, I believe the bar for success is now considerably higher. Until there is clear evidence of traction at Kate Spade, I see the shares as fairly valued.

data.title: Dividend Champion, Contender, And Challenger Highlights: Week Of October 5
data.description:
Introduction

The Dividend Champions list is a monthly compilation of companies which have consistently increased their annual dividend payouts. However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with around 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. In this weekly series, I highlight recent and upcoming dividend-related activity for dividend stocks on the Dividend Champions list.

In the data presented below, Yield is forward annualized and Years reflects the up-to-date streak, including dividends declared since the last edition of the Dividend Champions list.

data.title: Canadian Solar: Fade The Rally As Structural Headwinds Remain
data.description:
I previously covered Canadian Solar (NASDAQ:CSIQ) in June 2025, discussing how the solar industry had faced further recovery headwinds, attributed to the 55% China import tariffs, the polysilicon oversupply, and the impacted module pricing.

While the management had highlighted further reshoring efforts from 2026 onwards, these had already triggered the higher capex, the negative FCF, and the deteriorating balance sheet, worsened by the fact that the solar downturn was unlikely to lift in the near term attributed to the at-risk solar incentives.

In this article, I shall discuss how CSIQ's headwinds have further deepened, as observed in the mixed FQ3 2025 guidance, the bloated debt from the growing solar/energy storage assets, and the higher US-based capex, along with the ongoing cash burn observed in Recurrent Energy.

Combined with the structural risks observed in the solar industry and the mixed technical indicators, I believe that there may be more risks tied to its investment thesis ahead, warranting a reiterated Hold rating.

CSIQ Faces Uncertain Recovery Prospects, Attributed To Structural Solar Headwinds & Mixed Bottom-Line Performance

CSIQ 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Hold rating, CSIQ has been mostly trading sideways between the established resistance levels of $13s and the support levels of $9s, with it apparent that the structural headwinds in the solar sector remain elevated.

This is especially since the new US administration has tightened federal permits for future renewable projects, with it building upon the previously imposed tariffs on solar panel imports.

While CSIQ has strategically invested in solar module manufacturing capacity of 5 GW and utility-scale battery energy storage capacity of 3 GWh within the US, it remains to be seen if the company may claim 45x manufacturing credits moving forward.

My caution is attributed to the diversified global operations (mostly in China) and the mixed corporate structure/equity ownership in its China-based subsidiaries, with it uncertain if the management's eligibility assessment of the new Foreign Entity of Concern [FEOC] requirement may pass successfully.

This is on top of the fact that CSIQ's US-based solar module manufacturing capacity remains minuscule at 8.1% of its global capability at 61 GW and battery manufacturing capacity at 10% of 30 GWh, with it apparent that the solar company is likely to face tariff and demand headwinds moving forward.

These reasons may also be why the management already aims to invest $1.2B in FY2025 capex (+9% YoY) to boost its US manufacturing capacity.

These efforts are likely to be accretive to CSIQ's long-term prospects while allowing them to service the confirmed FQ2 2025 solar module backlog of 547 MWp (-3.1% QoQ/+144.1% YoY) and the battery energy storage backlog of 200 MWh (-75% QoQ/-66.6% YoY) in the North American region.

This is while servicing the international solar module backlog of 3,615 MWp (-6.9% QoQ/-20.9% YoY) and battery energy storage backlog of 5,084 MWh (-28.1% QoQ/+24.8% YoY) from its international manufacturing capacities.

It is apparent from these developments that CSIQ's global manufacturing strategy has been extremely prudent to directly serve regional demands, with it also tempering risks of footprint concentration.

On the other hand, given the ongoing structural headwinds, I urge investors to temper their near-term expectations despite the near-term tailwinds arising from the higher polysilicon spot prices arising from the much needed government intervention in China and the supposedly higher solar module pricing.

My caution is attributed to CSIQ's normalizing storage margins and the tapering of the leasing carbonate price benefit from henceforth, as hinted by the management's mixed FQ3 2025 overall revenue guidance of $1.4B at the midpoint (-11.2% QoQ/-6.6% YoY) and the lower gross margins of 15% (-14.8 points QoQ/-1.4 YoY).

This is worsened by the impacted net income performance in H1 2025, attributed to the growing annualized net interest expenses of $139.52M in FQ2 2025 (+22.8% QoQ/+84.5% YoY) arising from the higher net debt reliance at -$3.87B (-8.4% QoQ/-89.7% YoY).

These reasons are also why CSIQ's intensified US-based capex plans at a time of expensive borrowing cost environment are likely to trigger further balance sheet and bottom-line headwinds, pending the reversal in its free cash flow performance compared to the -$504.74M observed in H1 2025 (+63.7% YoY).

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Therefore, while the consensus forward estimates appear somewhat promising, with CSIQ's FY 2025 top-line performance expected to be a trough year before things drastically improve from FY2026 onwards, its path to profitability is likely to be prolonged, as observed in the consensus forward estimates above.

While the Recurrent Energy segment has been reporting growing solar/energy storage assets and partnerships with numerous utility players/companies, it has also come with expensive operational costs and debt, with it apparent that the yet profitable segment is likely to be continually subsidized by the CSI solar segment in the intermediate term.

Combined with the structural headwinds observed in the solar sector, I believe that the CSIQ stock is likely to continue trading sideways despite the inherent undervaluation to the growing Book Value per share of $42.50 in FQ2 2025 (+6.2% YoY) and the rich contracted backlog/long-term service agreement at $3B.

So, Is CSIQ Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

CSIQ 5Y Stock Price

For now, CSIQ has notably bottomed by April 2025 while charting an impressive rally of +98.4% by the time of writing, as the stock also consolidates above its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages.

CSIQ Valuations

The relatively fast/furious rally may also be why CSIQ appears to be somewhat inflated at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.18x, up notably from the 5Y mean of 0.69x and the 10Y mean of 0.68x, albeit still somewhat reasonable after comparing to some of its solar panel producer peers, including:

  • Jinko Solar at 0.92x,
  • JA Solar at 1.00x,
  • Trina Solar at 0.95x,
  • Longi Green Energy Technology at 1.41x,
  • Tongwei at 1.71x,
  • GCL Technology Holdings (OTCPK:GCPEF) at 3.37x, and
  • First Solar (FSLR) at 4.50x.

The same may also be observed in the relatively compelling FY2027 EV/Sales valuations of 0.85x, based on the Enterprise Value of $6.85B at the time of writing and the consensus FY2027 sales estimates of $8.02B.

Does this mean that I am upgrading the CSIQ stock to a Buy here?

Not quite.

While the EIA already expects the solar technology "to account for 80% of the growth in global renewable capacity" between 2024 and 2030, with it highlighting CSIQ's robust long-term growth prospects, I am of the opinion that the stock presents a mixed investment thesis attributed to the structural headwinds observed in the solar sector, the bloated debt, and the inherent lack of profitability.

CSIQ's Peaking Technical Indicators

At the same time, thanks to the peaking technical indicators, the implied overbought levels after the double digits rally, and the recent failure to break out of the 2025 resistance levels of $13s, I believe that CSIQ is likely to return part of its recent gains to retest the intermediate support levels of $11s, if not the August 2025 support of $9s.

Given the -23.3% downside at the midpoint from current levels and the notably elevated short interest ratio of 19.3%, there may be more risks indeed.

Conclusion

As a result, I maintain that CSIQ's investment thesis remains rather mixed, attributed to the underwhelming FQ3 2025 guidance, the bloated debt and, consequently, the hefty interest expenses, along with the inherent lack of profitability for the foreseeable future, as discussed above.

These reasons are also why I prefer to reiterate my Hold (Neutral) rating here, since it remains to be seen when the structural risks observed in the solar industry may lift.

Moving forward, readers may also want to observe how the US and Chinese governments tamp down on the polysilicon/solar module supply glut, since the new pricing policy will directly affect the solar industry supply chain while reshaping the investment cycle over the next few years.

For now, some patience may be more prudent.

data.title: AI: The Bubble Driving The Economy And The Markets
data.description:
Investors got a bit of a break on Friday as the September BLS report has been shelved until the government shutdown ends. Given the dismal nature of Wednesday's September ADP jobs report (32,000 jobs lost) and a nearly as abysmal August BLS jobs reading (22,000 jobs created, far under 75,000 expectations), investors can temporarily dodge another data point around the clearly faltering jobs market.

Instead, the AI revolution is happy for the moment to supply the hopium to send the markets ever higher. Today, Alphabet (GOOG) just announced another massive data center project. The $4 billion effort will happen on a 1,000-acre site just outside of Memphis, TN. Electricity for this huge facility will be supplied by utility giant Entergy (ETR). Both parties will magnanimously provide some $1.1 billion over the life of the contract to offset higher electricity costs for the citizens of the region.

Usually, the substantial job market reversal we have seen in recent months would send the stock market down along with yields on treasuries, as it portends a growing risk of recession on the horizon. However, equities just continue to advance. The yield on the 10-Year Treasury also remains firmly above the 4% level despite the prospect of further cuts to the Fed Funds rate on the horizon.

The chart above shows how unusual this trading action is from a historical perspective. The divergence between U.S. job openings and S&P 500 performance has never been this wide since records started being kept. As my regular readers know, I believe five of the most dangerous words in the English language are: It is different this time.

Equities have had a massive run since the beginning of 2023, with the NASDAQ up over 125% over that time. However, the majority of this performance has come from a small collection of tech giants like Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta Platforms (META), which are directing hundreds of billions of dollars from their capex budgets to building out AI capabilities and infrastructure. Most of this is going to construct massive electricity- and water-sucking AI data centers and the components that go in them.

Another sign of the disconnect between jobs and stock market performance is this. Approximately 60% of the returns in the stock market year-to-date have come from the Magnificent Seven, even with some outperformance from the S&P 493 over the past month or so. However, these seven companies only employ approximately 1.6 million people, roughly 1% of the U.S. workforce.

As can be seen in the chart above, almost all the earnings growth from the S&P 500 in the third quarter is projected to come from the Magnificent Seven. Earnings growth from the S&P 493 is currently estimated to be just less than 2% in Q3. But not to worry, analyst firms have the earnings growth from the S&P 493 in the low to mid-teens in the three upcoming quarters. That seems quite unlikely given tariff impacts on profit margins, a moribund housing sector, and a faltering jobs market.

As can be seen from the chart above, most of the current GDP growth is being powered by the AI Revolution in the form of a surge in tech spending. Robust spending by the defense industry and massive fiscal deficit spending (unsustainable) are also contributing to GDP growth.

However, the main economic driver in the U.S. economy is being provided via the AI revolution. Morgan Stanley recently projected that the transformative potential of generative AI will require roughly $2.9 trillion of global data center spending through 2028. This will consist of $1.6 trillion on hardware components like chips and servers, with the remaining $1.3 trillion for infrastructure. The rally in the market this tech spending has driven now has the overall market trading at a Shiller PE ratio of above 40.

How extreme is that valuation? It has been only broached one other time in U.S. history. That was the period from January 1999 through September 2000. This was during the last year of the Internet Boom blowout and the early innings of the Internet Bust. That bear market would take the NASDAQ down over 80% from its peak to the eventual equity bottom in August 2002. The S&P 500 fell just over 40%.

And unfortunately, the economic environment and backdrop currently are not nearly as favorable as they were in 1999, as can be seen above. A recent article noted that Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and Tesla (TSLA) combined will have spent $560 billion on AI-related capital expenditures since the beginning of 2024. For that, those investments have only brought in a collective $35 billion in AI-related revenue. Given that minimal return, how long will the tech giants continue to massively increase their capex budgets for AI-related projects?

My fear is at some point, AI will be unable to supply the next needed hit of hopium for the markets and the economy to get their next highs. At that point, both could experience a nasty bout of withdrawal symptoms. This in turn could leave the economy and markets feeling more than strung out.

data.title: NVDX: Leveraged Strategy For Secular Growth
data.description:
The T-REX 2X Long NVIDIA Daily Target ETF (BATS:NVDX) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of Nvidia (NVDA) shares. With the outlook for AI infrastructure investing remaining optimistic, I believe NVDX can be an appealing trading strategy for those seeking amplified returns over the underlying asset. Given that NVDX is a leveraged fund, traders should only consider the ETF for daily exposure as longer holding periods may result in adverse performance results.

You can review my most recent coverage of Nvidia here:

"Nvidia Is Still In The Early Innings Of The AI Revolution."

About T-REX 2X Long NVIDIA Daily Target ETF

NVDX was launched by Rex Shares October 19, 2023 on the CBOE Exchange. The strategy was designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of NVDA shares, amplifying exposure and potential returns for those seeking increased risk. NVDX can be an appealing trading strategy given its market depth; the fund has $659mm in net assets with an average of $212mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing traders substantial liquidity for active management. NVDX charges a modest fee relative to peer leveraged strategies of 105bps, potentially making NVDX one of the more optimal ETFs for active exposure.

The strategy was designed to provide 200% daily exposure to NVDA shares by investing in swaps and/or call options on NVDA shares. The strategy may also directly invest in NVDA shares, though the portfolio does not currently hold direct exposure to the stock.

Swap contracts were designed to provide the purchaser exposure to the total returns of the underlying asset, allowing for the fund to amplify exposure beyond the net assets of the portfolio. In order to gain leveraged exposure to NVDA shares, NVDX holds both receiver and payer swap contracts on NVDA shares.

Swap contracts have two parties: the receiver and the payer. The receiver essentially takes a long position in the underlying asset, receiving the returns of NVDA shares. The payer is the counterparty in the contract, paying the daily returns of the underlying asset while receiving a fixed rate. Swap contracts are settled on a daily basis, resulting in performance resetting daily. This means that performance is measured from day-to-day as opposed to rolling returns. This feature results in a daily reset of performance on the ETF, NVDX, allowing performance to be amplified, though reset on a daily basis.

As a result of the daily reset, NVDX will generally only provide 2x the daily performance of NVDA shares. Given the daily reset feature, longer holding periods can result in compounded returns, whether to the upside or downside, and can potentially result in value decay that may not be recoverable. As a result of this, traders should only consider holding NVDX for daily exposure as longer holding periods may result in adverse performance.

The 2x daily performance target is not guaranteed and may vary from day to day depending on a variety of factors, including volatility, liquidity, and daily trading activity.

During longer holding periods, investors can expect returns to be compounded, resulting in returns above the 2x daily target. This can potentially result in substantial gains or losses, depending on the investor’s holding period.

As a result of this feature, long-term investors must consider the risk of holding leveraged strategies for extended holding periods. Performance can be substantially amplified and may result in adverse performance during a bear market. NVDX performance will be exceptionally amplified during consecutive trading periods to the upside or downside. In some instances, performance can moderate during extended holding periods depending on the underlying asset’s trading performance.

In reference to Nvidia, I believe shares have significant potential for continued growth. One of the key factors that I’m looking for is the capital outlay across the hyperscalers, a strong indicator for the sales outlook for Nvidia given its prominence in AI compute and networking infrastructure. I believe the driving factor for the increased capital outlay is the growing backlogs across the hyperscalers, or the cloud compute capacity demand that cannot currently be serviced. Given the outlook for increased capital spend, I believe Nvidia will realize substantial revenue growth, particularly as more enterprises adopt GenAI and agentic AI to automate functions, from internal administrative processes to optimizing a sales desk.

As a leveraged strategy, NVDX can provide traders with increased exposure to NVDA shares to amplify potential returns. Though I cannot advise holding NVDX beyond a single trading day given the heightened risks, I believe NVDX can be used as a risk-on asset for those seeking returns beyond NVDA shares.

Risks Related to NVDX

NVDX is a leveraged strategy designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of NVDA shares, exposing traders to certain risks that must be considered prior to making an investment decision. Daily performance of NVDX is not guaranteed and may deviate from the intended 2x target. Holding NVDX for longer than a single trading period may result in compounded returns as well as significant value decay that may not be recoverable.

Liquidity can be a major factor that can impact the performance of NVDX. NVDX is dependent on the performance of NVDA, the performance of its derivative positions, and may be exposed to counterparty risk.

Traders must consider the all-in fees prior to trading NVDX. NVDX has a 105bps expense ratio that may impact the total returns of an active trader. Traders must also consider the tax implications when trading NVDX as short-term capital gains may impact their overall performance.

Prior to trading NVDX, I believe traders should exhibit significant trading discipline, particularly when it comes to cutting a position. Given that performance is amplified relative to NVDA shares, losses may mount beyond a trader’s risk parameters.

You can review additional risks here and here.

Final Thoughts

NVDX can be an appealing trading vehicle for those seeking amplified exposure to NVDA shares. Given my bullish outlook on NVDA shares, I believe traders can consider NVDX as an alternative strategy to increase risk exposure to NVDA shares. Given the significant risks involved when investing in leveraged strategies, I can only recommend the use of NVDX to seasoned traders and those that fully understand the implications of trading a leveraged strategy. I am recommending NVDX with a Buy rating.

data.title: Tamarack Valley Energy: Another Solid Clearwater Producer
data.description:
Tamarack Valley Energy, Ltd. (OTCPK:TNEYF) last ran a loss from all the acquisitions made. This was covered in the last article about a year ago. Generally, that is caused by the acquisition accounting. It is a combination of nonrecurring charges (usually due to the acquisition) combined with the desire by many to make as much of the acquisition costs depreciable as possible. Since then, the company has whipped the whole operation into shape and is now moving forward with the operations towards a more profitable future.

Areas Of Operation

This Canadian producer (that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted) grew by acquisition. That differs from many of the Clearwater operators that chose to grow through organic growth. The accounting loss at first implied "high costs" to many. But that was just the accounting choices made. If the company can grow what it acquired (and it appears to be doing just that), then there is a whole lot of low-cost growth to be obtained from those acquisitions.

Note that the heavy oil plays are not located anything close to the Cardium in the slide above. This is a rather recent play revolutionized by technology advances that have enabled some very cheap heavy oil production.

What made the acquisition strategy possible is shown above. Not all of that was known at the time of the acquisition. This is especially true of anything outside of Martin Hills. Furthermore, more intervals have become productive since then. The advancing technology keeps offsetting the premium that this company "paid to play."

The other thing is that almost no basins have the economics shown above which is featured by very fast payback periods and then multiple payback periods after that.

The best plays in the United States maybe get two payback periods in the same analysis.

But that means that if the heavy oil production has to be shut-in during a downturn, the company often has at least one payback period before the shut in happens. The hedging program could well obtain another payback whether or not production gets shut in. Even for an acquisition strategy that is a fair amount of profitability.

Pricing

One of the bullish stories revolves around pricing ever since the Trans Mountain Expansion Pipeline began operations. This alone tightened heavy oil and thermal oil supplies enough to reign in a discount that had periodically wreaked havoc on this part of the industry for as long as I can remember.

Obviously right now the current pricing situation is in a good place. The thing to remember here is that 2030 is a very long-time away and everyone in the industry sees that graph on the right-hand side shown above.

With that in mind, commodity industries generally overestimate the current forecast situation to bring about oversupply and another cyclical downturn. It would be far from the first time that happened.

Therefore, it is probably best to look at the current situation as a cyclical high unless something material happens to change that view. The idea that "this time it is different" is something I have heard during just about every recovery and market top cycle I can remember. Unfortunately, they were all followed by cyclical downturns. Right now, I do not see any reason for a material change in industry behavior.

Debt

The debt has been largely restructured with the extension of the bank line.

The latest earnings report has the net debt multiple at 0.7 which is considered more than satisfactory.

The company also agreed (since the second quarter earnings report) to sell some noncore assets. That money will likely be collected before the end of the fiscal year and be applied to the debt balances shown above.

To back up that net debt multiple, the latest earnings report also shows a generous amount of free cash flow:

Free funds flow shows plenty of money available for shareholder returns and debt repayments. Part of the reason for this is the increasing amount of production that is under waterflood plans. This part of the production has very low decline rates (and hence generates a lot of free cash flow).

But the other part is the very low cost and fast paybacks shown before.

Notice also that with the majority of acquisitions now about a year behind the company, the income statement is now reporting income.

Charlie Lake

The company also has some light oil production. This may be needed if the cyclical downturn is ever severe enough so that the discount expands to the point that production needs to be shut in.

It speaks volumes that this light oil play can compete at all with the economics of Clearwater. But this is a known very low breakeven play in Canada. it also makes sense that the company would grow production here to sort of "keep up" with the heavy oil production growth "just in case" the cash flow here becomes a priority in a cyclical downturn.

Summary

Investors should expect this strong buy idea to continue to optimize the portfolio with small acquisitions and noncore sales from time to time. This company grew rapidly through acquisitions. Now it appears to be successfully combining the whole situation into a very effective and efficient operation.

Debt is well within manageable range. Although usually heavy oil producers run with close to a clean balance sheet because of the discount to light oil and the cyclical nature of the upstream industry.

I suspect that the emphasis going forward will be on organic growth with an occasional opportunistic acquisition.

The company needs a longer track record as currently composed. But management does not appear to need to do anything extraordinary to accomplish that goal. Therefore, a price-earnings ratio expansion as that record becomes established is a reasonable goal. How far that expansion happens depends upon the market conditions "when we get there." But overall, the return should be worth the effort.

Risks

The heavy oil business always has the risk of a discount expansion to the point that production needs to be shut-in to avoid negative cash flow. Supposedly, Clearwater costs are low enough to avoid that. But that has yet to be demonstrated with a moderate to severe cyclical downturn.

A shut-in of production without sufficient hedging could cause a very reasonable debt ratio to become a challenge. This might cause material balance sheet damage or worse.

The export strategy could fail to meet profit expectations at any time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AppLovin: The Party Is Nearing Its End (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
AppLovin's Rally Should Take A Break Soon

You've probably heard of AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) before. It's a $238 billion market cap firm that built a software-based platform for advertisers that enhances their marketing and content monetization in the United States (and other countries). APP is already a multibagger of the past few years, and I covered it multiple times on Seeking Alpha. By the way, since my February 2025 bullish call, APP managed to go up by over 111%, already reaching my 12-month target (and it even exceeded it, to be honest), and also massively beating the S&P 500 index's (SPY) gain of <15% for the same period.

Based on what's expected from APP today, the firm's Q3 2025 earnings print next month is likely to beat consensus again. But I'm not so sure that the current stock's overvaluation leaves much room for further stock price appreciation. That great AppLovin's rally is definitely a result of the multiple expansions and strong guidance amid beating quarterly results that I was waiting for, but I think it's time for all AppLovin's shareholders to think twice about their capital protection.

In "overbought" times like today, it's probably a smarter choice to think of trimming, not buying more. That's why I'm withdrawing my previous "Buy" rating and downgrading AppLovin stock back to "Hold."

Why Do I Think So?

In Q2 (released in August 2025), AppLovin's sales soared by about 77% YoY and reached ~$1.26 billion, beating the consensus estimate by $38.5 million, or 3.15%, based on Seeking Alpha data. Their "net revenue per installation went up by 70% and the volume of installations increased by 8%," according to their 10-Q, and so the underlying growth of their business looks organic and real.

After they divested the apps business, they started to report for a single operating segment. In my view, this has made our lives (i.e., analysts' lives) much easier. At least now we can clearly see the net benefits of running the ad business alone. For example, their adjusted EBITDA margin managed to increase from 58% in Q1 2023 to 81% as of Q2 2025, and this "peak" above 80% isn't rolling down just yet.

And based on the CEO's commentary on the earnings call and at the Goldman Sachs conference, APP's MAX mediation platform, which "sits at the heart of AppLovin's ad tech stack," can sustain its double-digit growth and outpace the broader in-app purchasing gaming market's expansion. I think it's true because, according to the report prepared by Allied Market Research's team, this market's CAGR should be ~12.5% for the next 7-8 years, and it should eventually reach over $570 billion by 2032. So, the forward organic growth opportunity for AppLovin is still there, and we can't deny it, so the management's confidence in sustaining 20%-30% YoY growth in gaming alone seems to be justified indeed, in my understanding.

But I think that AppLovin wouldn't be worth over $200 billion in terms of its market capitalization if the gaming niche were its main end market (even growing by 20%-30% annually).

The management has been preparing for the new AXON ads manager roll-out, which was going to become a self-serve platform targeting e-commerce and web advertisers - based on this description, it was supposed to penetrate far beyond gaming in the coming years.

AppLovin finally presented AXON ads manager on Oct. 1, 2025:

Today, Oct. 1, we’re rebranding our customer-facing advertising offerings as Axon and opening our self-serve Axon Ads Manager on a referral basis. This is the next chapter in our journey: the same platform that has powered outcomes for the most sophisticated mobile gaming performance marketers, now delivered in a product that is simple, transparent, and built for businesses of every size. In the months ahead, we’ll use feedback from our referral-based opening to fine-tune the product for our broader public launch next year.

And as I can see, APP is running a pretty smart referral campaign to attract as many new clients as they can possibly serve:

With these generous ad credits they offered to advertisers, I think AppLovin will be able to guide for a higher sales figure at the upcoming earnings call.

The problem, however, lies in the recent earnings revisions, which seem to reflect the upcoming uptick in sales growth rates beyond the current fiscal year:

For the upcoming Q3 2025 (set to be released on Nov. 5, 2025), AppLovin's management guided for revenue of $1.33 billion at the midpoint and adjusted EBITDA of $1.08 billion (also at the midpoint), with margins remaining steady at 81%, like in Q1 and Q2. The analysts' consensus is for APP's adjusted EPS of ~$2.39 (+91% YoY), and revenue of $1.34 billion (+11.7% YoY), so they seem to be expecting the higher end of the management's ranges.

I believe AppLovin can beat even the current consensus revenue estimates. Also, if they keep the margins relatively stable, the same thing can happen in terms of the bottom-line consensus. But the stock is already priced to perfection, akin to its late 2024 valuation figures when I had a "Hold" rating on APP just because of that. With zero fundamental reason back then, the stock collapsed, and the real buying opportunity kicked in.

As far as I can see it, unfortunately for GARP investors, AppLovin is not going to get cheaper in the foreseeable future. Based on Seeking Alpha data, APP's FY2026 and FY2027 P/E ratios are ~50x and ~40x, respectively, and while the FY2026 EPS growth may indeed justify the FY2026 forward valuation, what comes next (+25% YoY in FY2027 EPS) seems to be insufficient.

Yes, the growth story is getting clearer now, the app business is gone, and the new AXOM ads manager platform has been rolled out. APP does require a higher-than-average valuation multiple for all the recent efforts, but while the early results may be promising in Q3/Q4, nobody canceled the potential issues when AppLovin leaps from hundreds to potentially thousands or tens of thousands of advertisers. I mean, the execution risks are still there, and the market seems to ignore them largely for now.

The Bottom Line

With all my love and long-term conviction for AppLovin and its growth story, I think its stock price has run far ahead of even the most bullish fundamental narratives. Priced at 75x forward earnings after the YTD rally of 111%, the margin of safety seems to be gone now, and any news of slower-than-expected adoption in new verticals, or a normalization of growth rates, could trigger a sharp re-rating to the downside.

The current technical picture suggests the same thing: After forming a so-called "gravestone doji" on a weekly chart, it appears to be ready to form this pattern's continuation (called "evening star").

Whatever the current candlestick pattern is called, it doesn't look bullish to me. I think the market may now punish APP for any minor misstep, sending it much lower under the pressure of its overvaluation.

But I'd be willing to take another look when they report in early November, or if APP dips before that by 15%-20% from the current price.

For now, I'm rating APP as a "Hold."

data.title: PLTW: Let Palantir Pay Your Weekly Bills
data.description:
Thesis

Single-name ETFs have become very popular in the past few years, with derivatives financial engineering being layered on top of classic equities in order to extract dividends. It is the case for Roundhill PLTR WeeklyPay ETF (BATS:PLTW), an exchange-traded fund launched in February 2025:

PLTW is designed for investors seeking a combination of income and growth potential. PLTW aims to provide weekly distributions and calendar week returns, before fees and expenses, equal to 1.2 times (120%) the calendar week total return of Palantir common shares (NYSE: PLTR). PLTW is an actively-managed ETF.

In today's article we are going to have a closer look at PLTW and its structure, look at its analytics and performance, and articulate our view on this innovative ETF.

Some analytics first

  • AUM: $0.19 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.99%
  • Distribution rate: 90% (will fluctuate)
  • Distribution frequency: weekly
  • SEC yield: 1.66%
  • Management style: active.

Fund composition

Let us start by looking at what the fund actually holds:

The ETF has an outright position in Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) stock, accounting for roughly 19.6% of the fund, with another large total return swap representing the rest of the holdings. Please note the two exposures add up to 120%.

Unlike some of its peers which employ derivative positions in order to extract dividends, this ETF has structured a swap with an investment bank in order to achieve the desired payoff profile.

Very robust performance so far

Ultimately "the proof is in the pudding", so let us have a look at how PLTW has done so far this year:

PLTW has delivered a robust +73% total return this year, beating PLTR and having a very close correlation to the name. While 120% of +66% would be +79%, there is some slippage in the total return because of the weekly goal for the 1.2x gearing ratio.

Please note we are running a "Total Return" graph here since the ETF has a very high distribution, as per its stated objective. If we ran just a price graph we would get the following:

A well structured fund will have a fairly stable NAV that flops around the 0% line, since the purpose of the structure is to disburse dividends in a flat or bull market. It is the case here, with PLTW exhibiting a fairly stable NAV given its gearing ratio of 1.2x and high distribution ratio.

The above graphs basically tell you that if you invested $100 in this ETF when it IPO-ed, you would have gotten $60 back already. This is a very important consideration when thinking about bear markets and investment amortization.

Suitability and structural considerations

While these types of buy-write funds usually attract an investor base that looks for dividends, there is also another aspect to consider which we shall discuss shortly.

Firstly, PLTW is addressed to those investors who like getting paid as frequently as possible, and most importantly to investors looking for dividends from equity exposures. It used to be that an investor needed to make a sale in order to harvest capital gains, but financial engineering now does that for you. PLTW is a slightly leveraged play on Palantir, and an instrument which delivers a weekly payout. This structure is great for people who look for a steady stream of income from a stock they like.

Secondly PLTW provides for an investment "amortization" schedule that does not require market timing. What does that mean? Well let us say that you invested $100 in this ETF when it IPO-ed. As of right now you would have gotten $60 back already, thus leaving your cost at 40c/$. This means that when the investment is fully 'amortized' an investor has little incentive to sell. Some retail investors buy momentum names only to panic sell at the bottom. It is not the case here with PLTW and its structure.

Risk factors

1) Single name exposure - this ETF purely employs financial engineering to get a weekly dividend payout from Palantir common stock. There is no diversification or other risk factors here. The fundamental performance for Palantir and the market reaction are the only considerations here. If Palantir keeps performing, this ETF will also gain. Conversely, if Palantir goes bankrupt, PLTW will follow suit, with a price that might hit a level close to zero (we are not saying this will happen, but investors need to understand the dependency in this outside case).

2) Gearing and drawdowns - the ETF has a 1.2x gearing ratio, thus will slightly outperform in bull markets but also experience deeper drawdowns during down markets. We have already witnessed a flavor of that during April 2025, when PLTW had a drawdown of almost -40% versus -34% for Palantir outright. The name will reflect the performance of the common stock with the added volatility from the gearing ratio. While we do not have one year of data yet, we can say with absolute certainty this is a high volatility ETF.

Conclusion

PLTW is a single name exchange-traded fund. The ETF represents a 1.2x weekly take on the Palantir common equity, and has convenient weekly dividends. If the Palantir common equity continues to appreciate an investor is looking at very generous weekly distributions. The fund is up +73% since its inception earlier in 2025, but has a substantial volatility profile and deep drawdowns. Investors here are simply transforming Palantir capital gains into weekly dividends, layered with the described gearing ratio on top.
